questions.question: Will the average global temperature in 2024 exceed 2023?

top_3.reasoning: Each of first 6 months of the year was above 2023 temperature by average of 0.47 F. There is a chance that due to El Ninio La ninia anomalies will trigger some cooling down but it would probably require to start in July  otherwise cooling in each subsequent month would need to be very significant vs 2023. And June 2024 was still 0.27F above June 2023. From the other hand July-Dec 2023 were extremly hot so going back even to 2022 temperatures would be enough to be below 2023.
top_3.why_selected: This rationale stands out for its strong quantitative reasoning and nuanced consideration of counterarguments. It calculates the exact temperature buffer 2024 has built while astutely noting that the extreme heat of late 2023 sets a high hurdle for the rest of the year. The forecast is well-calibrated, reflecting both the strong lead and the potential impact of an impending La Niña.
strengths: Strong quantitative reasoning
Considers historical baselines clearly
Well-calibrated uncertainty
weaknesses: Minor spelling errors
Lacks explicit citations for the exact temperature figures

top_3.reasoning: 2024 is tracking significantly hotter than 2023 in the first six months of the year according to the Global Climate Report  and experts assign a high probability that it will be the hottest year.   I am not as confident- the Global Climate Report shows a remarkably high temperature anomaly in the last six months of 2023. In fact this temperature anomaly is a bit higher than the one from the first six months of 2024. So temperatures need to still have a fairly high anomaly the rest of the year. This is very likely (90%).
top_3.why_selected: This rationale effectively synthesizes expert consensus with independent critical thinking. The forecaster correctly identifies the primary source of uncertainty—the exceptionally high temperature anomalies from late 2023—and adjusts their confidence accordingly. It provides a highly coherent and logical argument for a 90% probability.
strengths: Identifies the key comparative anomaly
Challenges expert consensus thoughtfully
Clear, concise logic
weaknesses: Lacks exact numbers for the anomalies discussed
Could elaborate more on what drives the expected remaining anomaly

top_3.reasoning: Every month since June 2023 - 13 months in a row - has ranked as the planets hottest since records began  compared with the corresponding month in previous years  the European Unions Copernicus Climate Change Service (C3S) said in a monthly bulletin. Reuters July 8th   Sea surface temperatures were record warm for the 15th consecutive month. NOAA Climate.gov July 16th  2024   Im at 96.5  not 100  only because the shift to LaNiña with the current temperatures and pressures  and over such a short transition time is unprecedented so the outcome is not known with certainty. Forecast for the next few months are for continuing record heat  so it would need to experience a very rapid cooling to offset the anomalous months that we have already experienced.
top_3.why_selected: This rationale provides excellent citations from credible sources, grounding its high probability in documented recent trends. It appropriately tempers absolute certainty by acknowledging the unprecedented nature of the current atmospheric shift to La Niña. The argument logically concludes that an extraordinarily rapid cooling would be required to change the outcome.
strengths: Excellent use of specific institutional sources
Highlights unprecedented nature of current transition
Clearly explains why certainty is not 100%
weaknesses: Slightly overconfident given the late-2023 anomaly factor
Punctuation and formatting are slightly messy

questions.selection_notes: Rationales 15 and 13 were very close for the top spot; both astutely recognized that the extreme heat of late 2023 makes the comparison closer than early 2024 data alone suggests. Rationale 15 narrowly took first place due to its specific quantitative estimates. Many other rationales correctly noted the 2024 lead but failed to account for the late-2023 baseline, leading to slight overconfidence.

questions.question: Will Elon Musk be the richest person in the world on December 31, 2025?

top_3.reasoning: Musk $249.9  Bezos 205.2B  Bernard Arnault 190.4B  Larry Ellison 173.3  Larry Page 151.8  Gates 135.2. Jen-Hsun (Nvidia). If Tesla drops from its current 250 to 150  Musk could easily drop a spot. Jen Hsun has seen a meteoric rise  that has stayed been volatile but somewhat plateaued for a month. Considering it has gone up 157% in the last year  a similar jump could see him topple Musk. Tesla has pressure from Chinese EVs. Metaculus is at 49% I will go a little higher at 60%. Musk is playing a shrewd election calculation. He is heavily and publicly supporting Trump. If Trump wins  he will have his ear (oops  bad pun). If Trump loses  Biden is pro EV anyway. He may expand the base of people willing to buy Teslas if the Trump supporters start to see it as desirable. His cybertruck has been a laughing stock among serious off road and rural people  maybe as much based on the radical style change as lack of practical bed use of a typical work truck. This is still a tough crowd to win. He also risks a backlash being too closely associated with Trump. Musks Teslas could go the way of the Hummer if they fall out of fashion on ideological/identity purchases.
top_3.why_selected: This rationale stands out for its comprehensive overview of the competitive landscape, providing exact net worth figures for top challengers. It effectively balances fundamental business risks with an insightful analysis of how political and cultural factors might impact Tesla's brand equity.
strengths: Provides precise net worth benchmarks for multiple competitors
Nuanced exploration of political and brand-identity risks
Incorporates community base rates into the forecast
weaknesses: Somewhat speculative regarding the exact impact of election outcomes on consumer behavior
Rambling structure in the second half

top_3.reasoning: #1 currently  by quite a bit.   I believe the index includes his Tesla pay package  which could be challenged in court again  but that probably wouldnt resolve by the end of 2025.   Biggest challenger is Bezos. Bezos has been selling some Amazon stock  whether for diversification  estate planning  or reinvestment into Blue Origin. Selling Amazon stock probably reduces the variance of his total net worth a year from now.
top_3.why_selected: This rationale is selected for its sharp, analytical points regarding variance and legal timelines. Noting that Bezos's stock sales mathematically reduce the variance of his net worth—thereby limiting his upside potential to catch Musk—is a highly sophisticated insight.
strengths: Identifies the crucial legal timeline for the $56 billion pay package
Sophisticated point about how diversification reduces competitor variance and upside
Well-calibrated to the base reality of Musk's current lead
weaknesses: Relatively brief
Omits discussion of Tesla's underlying business fundamentals

top_3.reasoning: Teslas share price has gone up recently  wiping out the losses from earlier this year. Musk won the trial about his pay package (USD 56bn) and is number one again  USD 55bn ahead of number two.  Things can change again and Tesla has more and more competition and an somewhat outdated portfolio (re: design) but for now things are looking good for him
top_3.why_selected: This rationale provides a concise and effective summary of the most critical drivers of the forecast. It correctly identifies the size of the gap and the importance of the pay package, while appropriately noting the counter-trend risks of Tesla's aging product lineup.
strengths: Directly quantifies the exact lead ($55 billion)
Balances recent stock recovery with structural business weaknesses
Clear and direct language
weaknesses: Lacks specific discussion of who the nearest competitors are
Does not explore the downside variance in as much detail as higher-ranked rationales

questions.selection_notes: There was a strong cluster of forecasts around 60%, reflecting a consensus that Musk's $50B lead makes him the favorite despite high volatility. Rationale 984 provided excellent quantitative data on Tesla's declining operating income but was heavily penalized for poor calibration, as a 16% forecast drastically overstates the likelihood of a massive wealth collapse within a relatively short timeframe.

questions.question: Will there be more than 25 named storms during Atlantic Hurricane Season?

top_3.reasoning: With 3 tropical storms so far and an average of 13.75 storms in the period between halfway through July and the end of the season  the base rate forecast using a Poisson distribution is roughly 1.4%.   Empirically this happened twice (2020 and 2005) in the past 20 seasons (10%) implying that the Poisson distribution is not the most accurate model. Hurricane seasons seem to have sort of a fat tail.   Meteorological agencies predict a very active hurricane season with a high number of named storms (22 according to UKMO  21 according to NOAA  26 according to TSR  25 according to CSU  23 according to UA). Averaging these forecasts and using the NOAA 70% confidence interval of 17-25 outputs a 27% prediction using a Poisson distribution  and about 30% using a normal distribution.
top_3.why_selected: This rationale rigorously aggregates five different institutional forecasts and models them using formal statistical distributions (Poisson and Normal). It effectively balances these models against the empirical base rate, insightfully acknowledging the presence of fat tails in hurricane occurrences.
strengths: Synthesizes multiple institutional forecasts
Applies robust statistical modeling to capture fat-tail risks
weaknesses: Relies on idealized statistical distributions that may not perfectly fit meteorological variables

top_3.reasoning: We are moving quickly from an El Niño to La Niña in the Pacific basin. NOAA is forecasting a range of 17 to 25 total named storms   Climatologically  we are transitioning from the homegrown storms of June and early July to the more long-track storms that roll off Africa and take days to cross the Atlantic. Saharan dust also reaches its peak concentration  helping to gobble up prospective storms.   This year  there’s also a third thing  the Madden-Julian Oscillation or MJO  a phase that promotes Pacific development and fills the Atlantic side with sinking air to further deter storm development.   The MJO is a cluster of clouds and rain that moves across the globe every 30-60 days. It can enhance or suppress tropical activity  depending upon what part of the world its currently in.   Flipping the switch   The opposite will be true heading into August. We are  of course  heading into the climatological peak of hurricane season  which will naturally promote storm development. In addition  the MJO will move back into a more favorable position for tropical development.   Im starting at 8. The NOAA forecasts are for a range of 17-25 and we had an early start to the season  but the lull  MJO and time between El Niño and La Niña raise high uncertainty. I am not comfortable to go up to the 46% forecast of Polymarket. It is far of of the NOAA estimate and as others have mentioned  there are only two previous years that have exceeded 25. [redacted to maintain anonymity] Part of this includes that localised changes that each system brings in terms of cooling or warming different regions and bringing more or less rain or pressure  to different areas within even countries the size of Mexico. These are not simple systems  especially when adding the general shift towards warming ocean temperatures. My guess is that it will be a severe and long season  but my uncertainty leads to fat tails. With global warming my default is to forecast higher than most estimates as it often turns out correct  but 21-25 captures the high end of the estimate  and the El Niño La Niña rapid shift may not moderate extreme temperatures. We are in the unknown without a clear base rate for this short of a transition combined with the already increased heat of the oceans.
top_3.why_selected: It offers the most detailed and concrete meteorological context, identifying specific short-term drivers (like the MJO and Saharan dust) that are suppressing current storm counts despite the macro-level transition to La Niña. The forecaster explicitly weighs uncertainties and tests their reasoning against external prediction markets.
strengths: Identifies specific short-term meteorological drivers
Explicitly considers uncertainty and evaluates crowd market discrepancies
weaknesses: Narrative-heavy with less direct quantitative bridging to the final 8% forecast

top_3.reasoning: Base rate--only two years ever  since they started recording storms  that weve had over 25. Still  both of those years were in the past 20 years. So possible. That said  normal start to the year with only 2 so far  and no storms predicted to form for the next 7 days which gets up until the end of July: https://www.nhc.noaa.gov/gtwo.php
top_3.why_selected: This rationale grounds its forecast securely in the historical base rate while incorporating immediate, decision-relevant evidence (the NHC's 7-day outlook) to update its probability. This demonstrates excellent situational awareness by proving why an extreme upper-tail event is mathematically harder to achieve given the slow July start.
strengths: Strong anchoring on historical base rates
Incorporates immediate, highly specific evidence (NHC 7-day outlook)
weaknesses: Forecast of 3% may be under-calibrated given the severity of institutional projections
May insufficiently weigh the highly favorable macro-conditions expected in August

questions.selection_notes: Rationales 574, 581, and 582 all effectively utilized NOAA's 17-25 storm forecast interval, but 574 was selected as the strongest for its rigorous aggregation of multiple external models. Rationales 579 and 582 were close calls for the third spot; 579 was ultimately chosen for its excellent use of immediate, short-term meteorological evidence (the NHC 7-day outlook) to inform the constraints on the upper tail, whereas 582 relied purely on an assumed symmetry of NOAA's confidence bounds.

questions.question: Will the majority of new cars sold be electric vehicles by the end of 2030?

top_3.reasoning: As far as I can tell  there is no explicit clarification on the manifold markets site whether or not Plug-in Hybrids count as electric vehicles for the purpose of this question. Im assuming they do not count and only purely electric cars with no combustion engines do.   Recent numbers from China have been very encouraging and given electromobility bulls new hope. China will almost certainly reach that threshhold way before 2030  but most other important markets  notably China and Europe (with big differences within countries) lag behind. In both  I see charging infrastructure as the major chokepoint - these countries have been slow to add large amounts of charging stations and could hardly even deal with that many electric vehicles being added to the fleet quickly. We also see protectionist measures aganst cheap Chinese EV imports  futher hampering sales growth in Europe.   Could it still happen? Yes. There have been substantial price drop in batteries  making electric cars more competitive. Ultimately  the trendline points to the 50% threshhold being reached  but my estimate is that it will be reached only by 2032 or 2033.
top_3.why_selected: This rationale provides a well-rounded assessment of the major drivers affecting EV adoption. It effectively balances bullish signals with concrete chokepoints like charging infrastructure and protectionist tariffs. The forecaster also demonstrates excellent calibration by acknowledging that the 50% threshold will likely be met, just a few years later than the 2030 deadline.
strengths: Balances both positive and negative drivers
Provides a specific alternative timeline (2032/2033)
Highlights geopolitical factors like tariffs
weaknesses: Makes an unverified assumption about the definition of EVs
Lacks specific numerical base rates

top_3.reasoning: The definition of cars and electric vehicles has not been made explicit.   The definition provided by the DoE is as follows:   An EV is defined as a vehicle that can be powered by an electric motor that draws electricity from a battery and is capable of being charged from an external source. An EV includes both a vehicle that can only be powered by an electric motor that draws electricity from a battery (all-electric vehicle) and a vehicle that can be powered by an electric motor that draws electricity from a battery and by an internal combustion engine (plug-in hybrid electric vehicle).   It therefore includes EVs and PHEVs.   This said this forecast depends on two main things: a) where are new cars being sold  and b) which types of cars are being sold.   Some of the largest markets (China  EU) are adopting more and more electric vehicles. Other large markets (US  India) are lagging.   OWID charts show that the current market share for EVs and PHEVs is about 18%. It has grown rapidly in the past 5 years  but it is not exploding exponentially.   I believe that one of the largest limiting factors here is on the supply side. Car manufacturers work with platforms that have an expected lifetime on the market of about 10-15 years. Given the fact that IC platforms most often cannot be easily used to manufacture EVs and PHEVs  as long as they are not substituted for new platforms  the share of electric vehicles on the market cannot reach very high levels.
top_3.why_selected: This rationale excels by establishing a clear definitional framework and anchoring its forecast to a concrete base rate (18%). It introduces a highly relevant supply-side constraint regarding the lifecycle of vehicle manufacturing platforms, adding a structural reason for why adoption cannot scale exponentially.
strengths: Establishes a clear operational definition
Anchors analysis in a specific historical base rate
Identifies a structural supply-side constraint
weaknesses: Lacks analysis of demand-side economic factors
Brief regional analysis lacks specific details

top_3.reasoning:
0. Yes to China no for the rest of the world.
1. Many to most older homes (in the US) cannot handle the amps needed to install a Level 2 charger without upgrading the wiring this is a significant cost overlooked by pundits; Compare/contrast the US with China---the skew of Chinese housing towards modern multi-family buildings makes converting to a charging infrastructure much more easier and cheaper. 2. Theoretically electric powertrains are a magnitude better than gasoline-engines in terms of maintenance and reliability---however in the real world the reliability of electric cars is (anecdotally) all over-the-map. The dominant failure point of electric vehicles is the motor and infotainment system which wholly depends on the quality of parts used and the competence of the assembly. 3. Expected resale value of electric vehicles in the US. One aspect that buoys the resale value of gasoline values in the US is that even a poorly maintained vehicle can be kept going by a 3rd or 4th owner with $500-worth of repairs on a semi-annual basis using aftermarket parts. With electric vehicles it is still uncertain whether for example the 4th owner (who makes $40 000 per year) of a Tesla 3 with 150 000 miles will be able to afford to use that car---anecdotes are all over the map with the most prominent example being a Tesla S owner who has driven >1 200 000 miles in his Tesla on the 4th battery pack and has gone through 14 motors. Severe depreciation of 10 year old EVs will have a cascade effect which will eventually affect original owners. 4. If there is a sustained and pronounced heat wave in the Northeast that pushes demand to the limits of the regional electric grid do not be surprised if an emergency measure is to forcibly throttle the electricity consumption of electric charging networks. Add the possibility of throttle/brownout anxiety to range anxiety. 5. Electric cars perform poorly during the extremes of northern US continental winters. (compare/contrast winter temperature range in the US Rust Belt versus Los Angeles London Shanghai).
top_3.why_selected: This rationale stands out for its high level of specificity regarding practical, real-world constraints. By exploring the hidden costs of home wiring upgrades and the economics of the secondary vehicle market, it provides a compelling case for why mass-market adoption may face significant hurdles.
strengths: Highlights specific, overlooked infrastructure costs
Insightful analysis of secondary market economics
Clearly structured and highly detailed
weaknesses: Relies heavily on anecdotal evidence for reliability claims
Overly focused on the US market

questions.selection_notes: Rows 44 and 59 were very close for the top spot; both addressed the definitional ambiguity surrounding PHEVs, though they made opposite assumptions and arrived at different numbers. Row 47 provided a solid list of factors but lacked the deeper exploration and clear structure found in the top three.

Im assuming they do not count and only purely electric cars with no combustion engines do. Recent numbers from China have been very encouraging and given electromobility bulls new hope. China will almost certainly reach that threshhold way before 2030 but most other important markets notably China and Europe (with big differences within countries) lag behind. In both I see charging infrastructure as the major chokepoint - these countries have been slow to add large amounts of charging stations and could hardly even deal with that many electric vehicles being added to the fleet quickly. We also see protectionist measures aganst cheap Chinese EV imports futher hampering sales growth in Europe. Could it still happen? Yes. There have been substantial price drop in batteries making electric cars more competitive. Ultimately the trendline points to the 50% threshhold being reached but my estimate is that it will be reached only by 2032 or 2033.", "why_selected": "This rationale provides a well-rounded assessment of the major drivers affecting EV adoption. The forecaster also demonstrates excellent calibration by acknowledging that the 50% threshold will likely be met, just a few years later than the 2030 deadline.", "strengths": [ "Balances both positive and negative drivers", "Provides a specific alternative timeline (2032/2033)", "Highlights geopolitical factors like tariffs" ], "weaknesses": [ "Makes an unverified assumption about the definition of EVs", "Lacks specific numerical base rates" ] }, { "rank": 2, "row_number": 44, "forecast": 0.25, "reasoning": "The definition of cars and electric vehicles has not been made explicit. The definition provided by the DoE is as follows: An EV is defined as a vehicle that can be powered by an electric motor that draws electricity from a battery and is capable of being charged from an external source. An EV includes both a vehicle that can only be powered by an electric motor that draws electricity from a battery (all-electric vehicle) and a vehicle that can be powered by an electric motor that draws electricity from a battery and by an internal combustion engine (plug-in hybrid electric vehicle). It therefore includes EVs and PHEVs. This said this forecast depends on two main things: a) where are new cars being sold and b) which types of cars are being sold. Some of the largest markets (China EU) are adopting more and more electric vehicles. Other large markets (US India) are lagging. OWID charts show that the current market share for EVs and PHEVs is about 18%. It has grown rapidly in the past 5 years but it is not exploding exponentially. I believe that one of the largest limiting factors here is on the supply side. Car manufacturers work with platforms that have an expected lifetime on the market of about 10-15 years. Given the fact that IC platforms most often cannot be easily used to manufacture EVs and PHEVs as long as they are not substituted for new platforms the share of electric vehicles on the market cannot reach very high levels.", "why_selected": "This rationale excels by establishing a clear definitional framework and anchoring its forecast to a concrete base rate (18%). Yes to China no for the rest of the world.
1. Many to most older homes (in the US) cannot handle the amps needed to install a Level 2 charger without upgrading the wiring this is a significant cost overlooked by pundits; Compare/contrast the US with China---the skew of Chinese housing towards modern multi-family buildings makes converting to a charging infrastructure much more easier and cheaper. 2. Theoretically electric powertrains are a magnitude better than gasoline-engines in terms of maintenance and reliability---however in the real world the reliability of electric cars is (anecdotally) all over-the-map. The dominant failure point of electric vehicles is the motor and infotainment system which wholly depends on the quality of parts used and the competence of the assembly. 3. Expected resale value of electric vehicles in the US. One aspect that buoys the resale value of gasoline values in the US is that even a poorly maintained vehicle can be kept going by a 3rd or 4th owner with $500-worth of repairs on a semi-annual basis using aftermarket parts. With electric vehicles it is still uncertain whether for example the 4th owner (who makes $40 000 per year) of a Tesla 3 with 150 000 miles will be able to afford to use that car---anecdotes are all over the map with the most prominent example being a Tesla S owner who has driven >1 200 000 miles in his Tesla on the 4th battery pack and has gone through 14 motors. Severe depreciation of 10 year old EVs will have a cascade effect which will eventually affect original owners. 4. If there is a sustained and pronounced heat wave in the Northeast that pushes demand to the limits of the regional electric grid do not be surprised if an emergency measure is to forcibly throttle the electricity consumption of electric charging networks. Add the possibility of throttle/brownout anxiety to range anxiety. 5. Electric cars perform poorly during the extremes of northern US continental winters. (compare/contrast winter temperature range in the US Rust Belt versus Los Angeles London Shanghai).", "why_selected": "This rationale stands out for its high level of specificity regarding practical, real-world constraints. questions.question: Will the U.S. win the gold medal in the Men's Basketball at the 2024 Summer Olympics in Paris?

top_3.reasoning: Prediction based on the probability implied by the bookies betting lines.   The crowd might be slightly overconfident. It is important to highlight the fact that the final phase of the tournament is knockout  so (unlike the typical NBA tournament where teams play 7 games) all it takes is a bad game to be out.
top_3.why_selected: This rationale perfectly anchors its forecast to the implied probability from betting markets while correctly identifying the primary structural risk. By highlighting the high variance of a single-elimination knockout format compared to an NBA playoff series, it justifies a realistic, well-calibrated probability that acknowledges potential crowd overconfidence.
strengths: Excellent calibration using betting markets
Identifies a crucial structural factor
weaknesses: Somewhat brief
Lacks specific analysis of opposing teams

top_3.reasoning: Despite American dominance at Olympics the US is not that dominating at World Championship. At Olympics half of the finals in 21st century were pretty close with a loss to Argentina in 2004. Notably the last final was US vs France and it was close. French as hosts can surprise this year. Case in point: Sure it was only a warm-up game but the Team USA has just beat South Sudan by one point 101:100. And South Sudan is not Argentina France or even Luthuania.
top_3.why_selected: This rationale provides a strong contrarian perspective by citing specific, recent empirical evidence and relevant historical context. It uses the recent one-point warm-up game against South Sudan and past Olympic struggles to justify a more cautious, lower forecast than the consensus.
strengths: Excellent use of recent concrete evidence
Good historical context
weaknesses: May overweigh the significance of a single warm-up game
Focuses mostly on downside risks

top_3.reasoning: US has one 7/8 Gold Medals in the pro era and definitely as the best team on paper. Stars seem to be motivated  which has been the main issue with past teams. Coming in around group consensus.
top_3.why_selected: This rationale effectively leverages a highly relevant base rate, noting that the U.S. has won seven of the eight gold medals in the pro era. It also addresses a key qualitative variable—star motivation—which has historically been a differentiating factor in U.S. Olympic performance.
strengths: Clear application of a relevant base rate
Addresses a key qualitative driver
weaknesses: Very brief
Heavily reliant on consensus

questions.selection_notes: Rationales 185, 187, and 192 were selected for their clear use of betting odds, recent evidence, and historical base rates, respectively. Rationale 188 was a close call due to its detailed analysis of the tournament draw, but its extreme forecast (96.7%) was poorly calibrated given the inherent variance of a single-elimination knockout format.
